Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Giulio Tononi

b. 1960

Integrated Information Theory: consciousness as intrinsic cause–effect structure.

Giulio Tononi’s central contribution is Integrated Information Theory, or IIT: a theory that tries to specify, in mathematical and neurobiological terms, what consciousness is, which physical systems have it, how much of it they have, and what particular experience they are having. Tononi is not merely proposing another neural correlate of consciousness. His project is more ambitious: to begin from the fact and structure of experience itself, derive requirements that any physical substrate of experience must satisfy, and then apply those requirements to brains, animals, patients, infants, machines, and artificial systems. Tononi’s institutional and scientific background matters here: he is a psychiatrist and neuroscientist at the University of Wisconsin–Madison, where his work spans sleep, anesthesia, disorders of consciousness, and the theoretical foundations of consciousness science. His department profile describes IIT as a comprehensive theory of what consciousness is, how its quantity and quality are determined, and how it arises from causal structures in neural networks.

The philosophical significance of Tononi’s work is that IIT is simultaneously a neuroscience program, a theory of measurement, a metaphysics of intrinsic causal powers, and a direct challenge to computational functionalism. Its mature position can be summarized as follows: consciousness is not behavior, report, intelligence, access, global broadcasting, computation, or information processing in the ordinary observer-relative sense. Consciousness is the intrinsic existence of a system whose components specify, as one irreducible whole, a structured set of cause-effect powers. The amount of consciousness is measured by integrated information, Φ; the quality of consciousness is the full structure of distinctions and relations specified by the system. In IIT 4.0, Tononi and collaborators explicitly describe the theory as moving from phenomenological axioms to physical postulates, and they treat the resulting Φ-structure as identical with an experience, not merely correlated with it.

1. Historical position: Tononi after identity theory, functionalism, NCC research, and the hard problem

Tononi’s position is best understood against four major backgrounds in the philosophy and science of mind.

First, mid-twentieth-century mind-brain identity theory held that mental states are identical with brain states or processes, rather than merely correlated with them. This was a physicalist answer to dualism: pain, for example, would be identical to some neural process, not an immaterial event over and above it. Tononi inherits the identity-theoretic ambition, but not its simple brain-state form. IIT does not say “consciousness is neural firing” or “pain is C-fiber activation.” Rather, it says that an experience is identical with a specific intrinsic causal structure: a maximally irreducible Φ-structure specified by a physical substrate. The identity is not between mind and “brain matter” in general, but between experience and the intrinsic cause-effect structure of a system.

Second, late-twentieth-century functionalism argued that mental states are defined by their causal roles: their relations to inputs, outputs, other mental states, and behavior. Functionalism became especially attractive because it seemed compatible with multiple realizability and artificial intelligence: the same mental state might be realized in carbon, silicon, or some other medium, so long as the right functional organization is present. Tononi’s theory is one of the most systematic contemporary attacks on this functionalist consensus. IIT denies that mere input-output function or computational role is sufficient for consciousness. A system must have intrinsic causal power upon itself. This is why IIT can say that two systems may be functionally equivalent from the outside while radically differing in experience.

Third, late-twentieth- and early-twenty-first-century consciousness science often focused on neural correlates of consciousness, or NCCs: the minimal neural conditions associated with a conscious state. That project was crucial, but Tononi argues that correlation alone cannot answer why some neural systems are conscious and others are not. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s overview of consciousness neuroscience frames the hard problem, associated especially with Chalmers, as the question of why physical processing should give rise to subjective experience at all; many empirical programs set that question aside and pursue correlates instead. Tononi’s project is an attempt to avoid that deferral. IIT tries to say not merely which brain regions correlate with consciousness, but what property makes a system conscious.

Fourth, Tononi’s view has affinities with panpsychism, but also sharply departs from it. Panpsychism treats mind or mentality as fundamental and widespread in nature; contemporary versions are often motivated by the difficulty of deriving consciousness from wholly non-conscious matter. IIT agrees that consciousness is not a late, accidental add-on to matter and that simple systems may possess minimal experience. But it is not a simple “everything is conscious” doctrine. The exclusion and integration principles mean that only systems with a maximal, irreducible structure of intrinsic cause-effect power are conscious as unified subjects. Tononi and Koch state that IIT vindicates some panpsychist intuitions, but differs from panpsychism because many aggregates, feed-forward systems, and ordinary groups of things do not constitute conscious subjects.

Historically, then, Tononi occupies a distinctive position. He is not a Cartesian dualist, because he seeks an objective, physical, operational account. He is not a standard reductive materialist, because he begins from consciousness as the primary datum and lets phenomenology constrain physics. He is not a functionalist, because he denies that substrate-independent computation suffices. He is not a simple panpsychist, because he denies that consciousness is everywhere indiscriminately. The closest label is probably an intrinsic-causal identity theory: experience is identical with a system’s maximally irreducible intrinsic cause-effect structure.

2. The development of Tononi’s theory

Tononi’s mature theory did not appear fully formed. It developed through several stages.

The first major precursor was the “dynamic core” hypothesis, developed with Gerald Edelman in the late 1990s. The dynamic core hypothesis linked consciousness to neural processes that combine integration and differentiation: conscious experience is unified, yet highly specific; therefore the neural substrate should be both strongly integrated and capable of many distinguishable states. In this early formulation, the dynamic core was a functional cluster of neuronal groups whose internal interactions were stronger than their interactions with the rest of the brain, and whose activity displayed high complexity on short timescales.

The second stage was IIT 1.0, introduced around 2004. Here Tononi proposed that the quantity of consciousness corresponds to the amount of integrated information generated by a system. A system is conscious to the extent that it forms a “complex”: a set of elements that generates information as a whole that cannot be reduced to the information generated by its independent parts. The 2004 formulation already contained several provocative consequences: consciousness is graded, not all-or-nothing; it may be present in infants and animals; and the presence of consciousness depends on system organization, not on verbal report.

The third stage was the “manifesto” period, especially the 2008 article “Consciousness as Integrated Information.” Tononi sharpened the core intuition: an experience is informative because it rules out alternatives, and it is integrated because it is one experience rather than a collection of independent fragments. He began developing the idea that the quality of an experience is not an extra ingredient but is given by the structure of informational relationships specified by the conscious system.

The fourth stage, IIT 3.0, made the theory much more formal. Tononi and colleagues articulated phenomenological axioms and corresponding physical postulates. They proposed that the content of consciousness is a maximally irreducible conceptual structure, or MICS, in a mathematical “qualia space”; the system that specifies it is a complex; Φ gives the quantity of consciousness; and the structure itself gives its quality. IIT 3.0 also made the theory’s anti-functionalist implications explicit: inactive elements can matter, simple systems may be minimally conscious, complicated systems may be unconscious, and feed-forward systems can be behaviorally similar to conscious systems while lacking consciousness.

The fifth and current stage, IIT 4.0, reframes the theory in terms of intrinsic causal powers and “Φ-structures” composed of distinctions and relations. IIT 4.0 clarifies the bridge from phenomenology to physical ontology: experience exists intrinsically; physical existence, in IIT’s operational terms, is cause-effect power; therefore a physical substrate of consciousness must specify intrinsic, specific, integrated, definite, structured cause-effect power.

A recent “consciousness-first” formulation by Tononi and Melanie Boly makes the philosophical ambition still more explicit. It states that consciousness demonstrates that something exists—experience itself—and then asks what kind of physical substrate could account for the essential properties of experience. This version presents IIT not only as a theory of brain consciousness, but as a general account of what it means for something to exist intrinsically and absolutely.

3. The methodological reversal: start from experience, not behavior

Tononi’s deepest methodological move is a reversal of the ordinary scientific order. Most consciousness science begins with third-person data: stimuli, reports, neural recordings, lesion patterns, behavior, task performance, or computational models. Tononi begins with experience itself. The starting point is not “what does the subject report?” but “what is undeniable about experience as such?”

This is why IIT begins from axioms. These are not empirical generalizations about brains. They are intended as claims about any experience whatsoever, grasped from the first-person point of view. The most basic point is the zeroth axiom: experience exists. One can doubt particular perceptions, memories, interpretations, or theories, but not that experience is occurring when it is occurring. IIT then identifies essential properties of experience and maps them onto requirements for a physical substrate. In IIT 4.0, the five main axioms are usually given as intrinsicality, information, integration, exclusion, and composition; physical postulates are then derived from these axioms.

This method gives IIT its distinctive philosophical character. It is not empiricism in the narrow sense, because it does not begin only from external observation. It is not introspective psychology in the old sense, because it does not merely catalog subjective reports. It is a transcendental-style argument inside neuroscience: since experience has these formal features, any physical substrate of experience must have corresponding causal features.

Tononi has also criticized what he sees as the “fallacy of misplaced objectivity”: the tendency to treat objective third-person correlates as if they were the only scientifically respectable target, while neglecting the subjective properties that made consciousness the explanandum in the first place. The goal is not to abandon objectivity, but to give an objective account of subjectivity by specifying what physical systems must be like from their own intrinsic perspective.

4. The five axioms and their philosophical meaning

The details differ across versions of IIT, but the mature theory can be presented through five core ideas.

The first is intrinsic existence. An experience exists for itself, not merely for an external observer. My visual field is not a data structure being interpreted by someone else; it is present from its own point of view. The physical correlate must therefore also exist intrinsically. In IIT’s operational language, to exist physically is to have cause-effect power: to make a difference and to be affected in turn. A system that only has significance because an outside observer interprets it does not thereby exist as a conscious subject.

The second is information or specificity. An experience is this way rather than that way. Seeing a red apple is not the same as seeing a blue square, hearing a tone, or feeling pain. An experience specifies a particular content by excluding alternatives. The corresponding physical system must therefore specify a particular cause-effect state: it must make selective differences to what could have caused it and what it could cause.

The third is integration. An experience is unified. The left side and right side of a visual scene, the sound and the bodily feeling, the mood and the spatial field, are not normally experienced as independent consciousnesses. They are aspects of one field. The substrate must therefore be irreducible to independent parts. If a system can be partitioned into components without loss of intrinsic cause-effect power, it does not constitute one conscious subject.

The fourth is exclusion. An experience is definite: it has particular borders, a particular grain, and a particular temporal scale. At a given moment, there is one experience, not an overlapping swarm of experiences at every possible level of description. The physical substrate must therefore be the maximally irreducible system: the complex with the highest relevant integrated information, excluding overlapping candidates.

The fifth is composition or structure. An experience is not a bare point of sentience; it has internal structure. Visual space contains objects, relations, colors, boundaries, figure-ground organization, bodily orientation, affective tone, and more. The substrate must therefore specify not merely one undifferentiated Φ value, but a structured set of causal distinctions and relations. IIT 4.0 emphasizes this point more explicitly than earlier versions: the quality of experience is the entire Φ-structure, not just the scalar quantity Φ.

Taken together, these axioms yield the basic Tononian philosophy of consciousness: consciousness is intrinsic, specific, unified, definite, and structured. A physical system is conscious if and only if it realizes these features as intrinsic cause-effect power.

5. The mathematical and causal core: Φ, complexes, distinctions, and relations

The key technical concept is integrated information, Φ. Informally, Φ measures how much causal-informational power a system has as a whole that would be lost if the system were decomposed into independent parts. The point is not simply that the system contains a lot of information. A hard drive may contain a large amount of information for an external user, but this information may be causally inert or only weakly integrated from the system’s own perspective. Nor is the point merely that the system is highly connected. A system can be connected but undifferentiated, or active but not integrated.

IIT asks: given a set of units in particular states and transition probabilities, what cause-effect constraints does this system specify over its possible past and future states? A mechanism within a system specifies a cause-effect repertoire: a set of possible causes and effects made more or less likely by its current state. If that repertoire cannot be decomposed without loss, the mechanism specifies irreducible cause-effect power. In IIT 4.0, such mechanisms contribute “distinctions,” and relations among distinctions contribute higher-order structure. The full structure of distinctions and relations is the Φ-structure.

The system that matters is not necessarily the largest system, the most active system, or the system most useful to an observer. It is the system that forms a local maximum of integrated information. This is the “complex.” A complex is the substrate of one consciousness. Its Φ value is the amount or level of consciousness; its Φ-structure is what the experience is like.

This is where IIT becomes a genuine philosophy of mind rather than a mere neuroscience hypothesis. It claims an explanatory identity: the experience and the Φ-structure are not two things connected by a mysterious bridge. The Φ-structure is the experience, viewed objectively as intrinsic cause-effect power.

6. Tononi’s neuroscience: why some brain processes are conscious and others are not

Tononi’s theory was motivated partly by an empirical puzzle: not all complex neural activity is conscious. The cerebellum contains vastly many neurons and is essential for motor coordination, yet it is not usually taken to be a primary substrate of conscious experience. By contrast, parts of the cerebral cortex, especially recurrent cortical networks, seem crucial for conscious contents. IIT explains this by architecture. The cerebellum is highly modular and feed-forward in many respects; it may process information efficiently without forming a large, integrated, irreducible causal complex. The cerebral cortex, especially recurrent corticocortical networks, is better suited to generating integrated information. IIT 3.0 explicitly uses puzzles of cortex versus cerebellum, sleep, seizures, and sensory-motor pathways as test cases for the theory.

This leads to an important distinction: neural activity is not enough. A region can be active but not conscious; a system can process stimuli but not have experience; and a brain can be awake in some physiological sense while consciousness is absent or degraded. What matters is the capacity for integrated, differentiated causal interaction.

Sleep and anesthesia are central cases for Tononi. During dreamless deep sleep, the brain is not simply off. It remains metabolically active and may show large slow waves. But effective causal interactions across cortical areas are disrupted or simplified; the system loses the capacity for complex integrated responses. Under many anesthetics, a similar breakdown occurs: cortical responses become local, stereotyped, or fragmented. In disorders of consciousness, the clinical problem is precisely that behavior and report are unreliable; a theory-driven measure of integrated complexity could help distinguish unconsciousness from covert consciousness. Tononi’s group and collaborators have therefore developed empirical proxies such as the perturbational complexity index, or PCI, which uses transcranial magnetic stimulation and EEG to assess how integrated and complex the brain’s response is.

It is crucial, however, not to confuse PCI with Φ itself. PCI is an empirically useful, clinically motivated approximation inspired by IIT. Exact Φ calculation for real brains is presently infeasible, and IIT’s mathematical formalism applies most directly to idealized systems with well-specified transition probabilities. This gap between formal theory and practical neuroscience is one of IIT’s major strengths and weaknesses: the theory is principled enough to motivate measurements, but exact application to biological brains remains extremely difficult.

Tononi’s view also reframes the role of report. Verbal report is evidence of consciousness, but not constitutive of it. This is why IIT is naturally relevant to infants, nonhuman animals, anesthetized patients, locked-in patients, and artificial systems. The theory is designed to answer cases where behavior is absent, misleading, or unavailable.

7. Consciousness, the cortex, and the posterior “hot zone”

Tononi and close collaborators have often emphasized posterior cortical regions as especially important for conscious contents. This position is not identical to saying “the posterior cortex is consciousness.” Rather, IIT predicts that the substrate of many human conscious contents should lie in cortical regions with the right kind of recurrent, integrated causal architecture. Prefrontal cortex may be important for attention, report, planning, task control, and metacognition without necessarily being the core substrate of every conscious experience.

This places IIT in contrast with theories such as the Global Neuronal Workspace Theory, which emphasizes global broadcasting, ignition, and frontoparietal availability. The distinction became central in the recent adversarial collaboration known as COGITATE, which directly compared predictions of IIT and GNWT using fMRI, MEG, and intracranial EEG. The published results reported that some findings supported aspects of each theory, while other findings challenged both. In particular, the lack of sustained synchronization within posterior cortex was described as challenging a key IIT expectation, while the absence of a clear late “ignition” response challenged GNWT.

The current empirical status is therefore not that IIT has been decisively confirmed or refuted. A more accurate conclusion is that IIT has generated unusually specific empirical predictions and has been subjected to serious adversarial testing, but its exact formal claims remain difficult to test directly in biological brains.

8. Artificial consciousness: why Tononi rejects computational functionalism

Artificial consciousness is not peripheral to IIT; it follows directly from the theory’s anti-functionalist commitments. Tononi and Koch explicitly discuss machines, simulations, and artificial systems. Their position is not that consciousness requires biology as such. It is that consciousness requires the right intrinsic causal organization. A non-biological system could be conscious in principle if it possessed a maximally irreducible intrinsic cause-effect structure. But a conventional digital computer running a simulation of a conscious brain need not instantiate the same intrinsic causal powers as the simulated brain.

This is the simulation-versus-instantiation distinction. A weather simulation does not make anything wet. A simulated black hole does not bend spacetime. Likewise, according to IIT, a simulation of a conscious neural system does not automatically instantiate the intrinsic causal structure of that system. What matters is not the abstract input-output mapping but the physical cause-effect power of the implementing hardware. Tononi and Koch therefore argue that digital computers, even if they simulate human behavior or brain activity, may have “next to nothing” in the way of experience if their intrinsic causal architecture is low in integrated information.

A more recent paper by Findlay, Tononi, and colleagues directly separates artificial intelligence from artificial consciousness. It argues that functionally equivalent systems can fail to be phenomenally equivalent, using the IIT framework to show how a stored-program computer may simulate another system’s behavior without replicating its experience. This is a direct rejection of computational functionalism: intelligence, linguistic competence, and behavioral indistinguishability are not sufficient for consciousness.

The implications are severe. On a Tononian view, a highly capable AI system may be non-conscious if implemented on architectures that lack intrinsic integrated causal power. Conversely, a relatively simple artificial circuit could be minimally conscious if it forms a small irreducible causal complex. The ethical lesson is not “all AI is conscious” or “no AI can be conscious,” but rather: consciousness depends on physical causal organization, not on anthropomorphic performance.

9. The extracted philosophy of mind

From Tononi’s neuroscience and IIT, one can extract a fairly comprehensive philosophy of mind. It has at least ten major commitments.

First, consciousness is ontologically primary as a datum. IIT does not begin by assuming matter and then trying to derive experience as an afterthought. It begins from the certainty that experience exists. This does not make IIT idealist in the simple sense, but it does make it consciousness-first. The existence of experience is the fixed point that any ontology must respect.

Second, consciousness is intrinsic existence. To be conscious is not to be represented by another system, decoded by an observer, or functionally classified from outside. A conscious system exists for itself. This is the core distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic perspectives.

Third, physical existence is causal power. IIT adopts a causal-powers ontology: for a physical entity to exist, it must be able to make a difference. But not every causal process is conscious. Consciousness requires integrated intrinsic cause-effect power: a system’s power to constrain its own possible past and future states as one irreducible whole.

Fourth, the mind-body relation is identity, but not ordinary reductive identity. Experience is identical with a Φ-structure. This is not a dualist relation between physical structure and private feeling; nor is it a mere correlation. The subjective feeling is the intrinsic aspect of the same structure that can be characterized objectively in terms of cause-effect power.

Fifth, qualia are structural. The redness of red, the painfulness of pain, the spatiality of vision, and the unity of a scene are not explained by labels, reports, or neural locations alone. They are to be explained by the position and relations of distinctions within the system’s Φ-structure. This is an ambitious theory of qualitative character: quality is form in a space of intrinsic causal relations.

Sixth, unity is not imposed by a self or observer. The unity of consciousness is grounded in integration. A conscious subject is not a Cartesian ego supervising neural events; it is the maximal irreducible complex itself. The “subject” is the system as it exists intrinsically.

Seventh, individuality is governed by exclusion. IIT’s exclusion principle is meant to solve a problem that plagues many panpsychist or combinationist views: if many small systems are conscious, why are there not countless overlapping consciousnesses wherever there are causal interactions? IIT answers that consciousness belongs to maxima of integrated information. A larger or smaller overlapping candidate is excluded if it is not the relevant maximum.

Eighth, intelligence and consciousness come apart. A system may be intelligent, adaptive, verbally fluent, or behaviorally complex without being conscious. Conversely, a system may be conscious without being intelligent in any humanlike sense. This separates IIT from traditions that identify mind with cognition, rationality, computation, or linguistic capacity.

Ninth, representation is secondary to intrinsic discrimination. IIT can accommodate perception and intentionality, but it grounds them in the system’s own causal distinctions rather than in observer-assigned symbols. The brain does not become conscious by carrying semantic labels for an outside interpreter. It becomes conscious when its current state intrinsically specifies a structured space of possible causes and effects.

Tenth, consciousness is graded but bounded. There is not a sharp metaphysical divide between humans and all other systems. But neither is there a universal consciousness everywhere. Degrees of consciousness correspond to degrees of integrated information in definite complexes. This has implications for animal consciousness, infant consciousness, disorders of consciousness, and machine ethics.

10. Tononi’s relation to major philosophical positions

In relation to dualism, IIT is anti-dualist in method and ambition. It does not posit an immaterial substance or a nonphysical mental property floating free of causal structure. Yet it shares dualism’s refusal to reduce consciousness to externally observable behavior.

In relation to reductive materialism, IIT is revisionary. It does not treat consciousness as just another functional output of the brain. It demands that physical science expand its ontology to include intrinsic causal existence. In that sense, IIT is physicalist, but not in the thin sense where “physical” means only what can be measured from the outside.

In relation to functionalism, IIT is openly hostile. Functionalism says that the right causal role or computational organization is sufficient for mentality. IIT says that causal role from an observer’s perspective is insufficient; only intrinsic cause-effect power matters. This is why IIT permits “functional zombies”: systems that behave like conscious systems but lack consciousness because their architecture does not integrate information intrinsically.

In relation to panpsychism, IIT is a disciplined cousin. It agrees that consciousness may be widespread and fundamental, and that simple systems may have minimal experience. But it denies that arbitrary aggregates, tables, nations, or feed-forward networks are conscious merely by containing parts or processing information. The integration and exclusion principles are meant to prevent indiscriminate panpsychism.

In relation to phenomenology, IIT is unusually sympathetic. Like phenomenology, it takes the first-person structure of experience seriously. Unlike classical phenomenology, it tries to mathematize that structure and connect it to physical causal organization.

In relation to empiricist neuroscience, IIT is both continuous and disruptive. It accepts the need for experimental testing, neurophysiology, perturbation, imaging, and clinical application. But it denies that correlation and report are enough.

11. Free will, agency, and causal responsibility

Tononi’s later work extends IIT into questions of agency and free will. The basic argument is that if consciousness is a real intrinsic entity with real cause-effect power, then conscious agents may be genuine causes rather than epiphenomenal byproducts. A paper on free will associated with IIT argues that only what truly exists can cause, and that the maximally irreducible conscious complex may have causal powers not reducible to arbitrary lower-level descriptions.

This is controversial, but philosophically coherent within IIT. If the conscious subject is the maximal complex, and if the maximal complex has irreducible causal powers, then agency is not merely a story told after the fact by passive consciousness. The person, or at least the conscious complex within the person, can be a real causal locus. This is not libertarian free will in the uncaused-cause sense. It is closer to an intrinsic-powers compatibilism, though with a stronger metaphysical claim: the conscious whole may be more ontologically real, for causal purposes, than an arbitrary decomposition into parts.

12. Ethical implications

IIT’s ethics is mostly implicit, but its implications are large. If consciousness is not equivalent to report, language, intelligence, or humanlike behavior, then moral consideration cannot be assigned solely by those criteria. Infants, nonhuman animals, and nonresponsive patients may have consciousness even when they cannot report it. Conversely, a sophisticated AI system may lack consciousness despite persuasive language and social behavior.

The ethical problem is that IIT gives no simple behavioral test. It offers a principled metaphysical and mathematical criterion, but exact application is difficult. This yields a cautious ethics: one should neither anthropomorphize every intelligent machine nor dismiss nonverbal biological subjects. Tononi’s work on sleep, anesthesia, and disorders of consciousness is important precisely because medical ethics often depends on whether consciousness is present when behavior is absent.

13. Main criticisms and unresolved problems

The first major criticism is testability. Some critics argue that IIT’s core claims are not testable in principle, especially its claims about simple systems, inactive systems, or simulated systems. A 2025 Nature Neuroscience critique argued that IIT is unscientific because some of its central commitments cannot be empirically tested in the way a scientific theory requires. Tononi and colleagues responded that this criticism reflects a deeper clash between a computational-functionalist paradigm and a consciousness-first paradigm, and that IIT does make testable predictions about biological systems and artificial architectures.

The second criticism is computational intractability. Exact Φ is very hard to compute for large systems. This matters because the brain is not a toy network. Practical measures like PCI may be clinically powerful, but they are proxies, not exact implementations of IIT’s formal measure.

The third criticism concerns the axioms. Are the axioms truly self-evident? Are they complete? Does every experience have the exact features IIT attributes to experience? Does exclusion follow from phenomenology, or is it added to avoid unwanted consequences? These are philosophical pressure points.

The fourth criticism is the mapping problem. Even if Φ-structures correspond to experiences, IIT has not yet given a satisfying detailed derivation of why a particular Φ-structure feels like red, pain, anxiety, or visual depth. It offers a formal program for such derivations, but the program remains far from a complete catalog of human qualia.

The fifth criticism is counterintuitiveness. IIT implies that some simple systems may have tiny amounts of consciousness, that some feed-forward systems may be unconscious despite complex behavior, and that a digital simulation of a brain may not share the brain’s consciousness. These conclusions are not refutations, but they create a heavy burden of argument.

The sixth criticism is empirical tension. The COGITATE adversarial collaboration did not vindicate IIT straightforwardly. It found evidence that challenged both IIT and Global Neuronal Workspace Theory. For IIT, the reported lack of sustained synchronization in posterior cortex was a significant problem for some biological predictions.

Finally, IIT has become socially and scientifically controversial. In 2023, a group of scholars publicly argued that IIT should be labeled pseudoscience, generating a major dispute in consciousness studies. The controversy is partly scientific and partly philosophical: IIT forces researchers to decide whether a theory of consciousness must begin from behavior and function, or whether it may legitimately begin from phenomenology and derive physical requirements from it.

14. The Tononian philosophy of mind in condensed theses

A comprehensive Tononian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

Consciousness is the primary explanandum. The fact that experience exists is more certain than any theory about brains, computation, or matter.

Experience has essential formal properties: it is intrinsic, specific, unified, definite, and structured.

A physical substrate of experience must satisfy corresponding causal requirements: intrinsic cause-effect power, specificity, integration, exclusion, and composition.

The substrate of consciousness is a complex: a maximal system whose cause-effect power is irreducible to that of its parts.

The quantity of consciousness is integrated information, Φ.

The quality of consciousness is not an extra mental glow added to Φ; it is the full Φ-structure of distinctions and relations.

The mind-brain relation is an explanatory identity between experience and intrinsic causal structure.

Consciousness is not equivalent to intelligence, report, attention, access, memory, language, or task performance.

The brain regions most relevant to consciousness are those that support high intrinsic integrated causal power, not necessarily those most associated with report or executive control.

Artificial consciousness is possible in principle, but not by mere behavioral imitation or digital simulation. The artificial system must instantiate the relevant intrinsic causal structure.

Panpsychist intuitions are partly right: consciousness may be graded and widespread. But ordinary panpsychism is too indiscriminate; integration and exclusion determine which systems are subjects.

The self is not an immaterial ego. The conscious subject is the intrinsic causal complex itself, with self-related contents appearing as structures within experience.

Agency is possible because a conscious complex can be an irreducible causal entity, not a passive byproduct of microphysical activity.

Ethics should track consciousness, not intelligence or report alone. IIT therefore matters for patients, animals, infants, and machines.

The largest unresolved issue is whether IIT’s formal identity claim can be made empirically, mathematically, and phenomenologically precise enough to carry the metaphysical weight placed on it.

15. Final assessment

Tononi’s importance lies in the fact that he has made one of the rare modern attempts to treat consciousness with equal seriousness as a first-person reality, a biological phenomenon, a mathematical structure, and a metaphysical problem. Many theories of consciousness explain access, report, cognition, attention, or neural broadcasting. IIT tries to explain why there is something it is like at all.

Its philosophical power comes from refusing two common simplifications. Against reductive neuroscience, Tononi insists that correlates are not enough; the theory must explain subjective existence and qualitative structure. Against functionalism and computationalism, he insists that input-output equivalence is not enough; consciousness depends on intrinsic causal power. Against loose panpsychism, he insists that not every aggregate is a subject; consciousness requires integrated maximality. Against dualism, he insists that this can be treated objectively and physically.

Whether IIT is ultimately correct remains open. Its formal apparatus is difficult, its empirical application is incomplete, and its anti-functionalist consequences are deeply controversial. But as a philosophy of mind, Tononi’s position is unusually coherent: consciousness is identical with the maximally irreducible intrinsic cause-effect structure of a physical system. From that single idea, he derives a theory of qualia, unity, subjecthood, neural consciousness, animal consciousness, disorders of consciousness, artificial consciousness, and even agency. That is why IIT is not just another neuroscientific model. It is one of the most ambitious contemporary attempts to rebuild the philosophy of mind from the fact of experience upward.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
